properties.snippet: This dataset displays information about the grants from the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy (EGLE)’s Renewables Ready Communities Awards (RRCA).
properties.description:
The Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy (EGLE) protects Michigan's environment and public health by managing air, water, land, and energy resources, and is focused on addressing climate change, diversity, equity, and inclusion. In 2023, Governor Gretchen Whitmer's budget allowed for state funds to reward renewable energy adopters in the state. As such, in fiscal year (FY) 2024, EGLE will provide $5,000 per megawatt (MW) to permitters and expectant hosts of eligibility utility-scale renewable energy projects which underwent local permitting processes, with $2,500 per MW granted to each awardee in cases when host and permitter differ. There is no deadline to apply to this solicitation. This award will remain open until funds are depleted.

This data displays each municipal grantee of the Renewables Ready Communities Awards (RRCA)with some selected fields of relevant information available by clicking each grantee. This data is visualized in a dashboard which summarizes some major statistics of the award, including funds currently disbursed, number of megawatts awarded, and number of municipalities awarded.Municipality locations are estimated and approximate. Many grant and loans are distributed to counties and municipalities without a precise location attached to them. In these cases, the center of the county or municipality was used for the point location on the map.

The data is expected to be updated alongside each round of the RRCA, which occurs roughly every two months and adds 5-10 grantees each round. For more information, please visit the Renewables Ready Communities Award webpage.

For questions about this data contact Ian O'LearyOLearyI@michigan.gov.

properties.snippet: The Michigan Radiation Environmental Monitoring Program (MREMP) has been collecting and analyzing environmental samples around facilities that emit or could emit significant quantities of radioactive material effluents since 1958. MREMP is operated pursuant to MCL 333.13518. Facilities that have been determined to fit the criteria for this program are commercial nuclear power plants. There are four properties that have operation or have operated commercial nuclear power plants and are commonly known as Cook, Fermi (1 and 2), Palisades, and Big Rock Point. Environmental monitoring is conducted via the collection and analysis of air, water, milk, and direct gamma measurements. These samples are analyzed for different types of radiation depending on the media type. The radiological results are provided in this GIS layer and contain results from 2017-Present. Historical data dating back to 1958 can be found on the MREMP website.
properties.description:
Environmental samples collected for MREMP are analyzed for different radionuclides depending on their type. The results are provided in table format and sorted by nuclear power plant. Samples are also collected in the Lansing area that are used for background monitoring and are not associated with a facility. Background measurements are useful when it may be necessary to determine if detections are significant. Milk, water, and air samples are analyzed by the radiological protection laboratory (RPL) using three techniques. These three techniques are gamma spectrometry (GS), gas flow proportional counting (GFPC), and liquid scintillation counting (LSC). A third-party analysis laboratory evaluates and provides results for direct gamma radiation readings of thermoluminescent dosimeters (TLD).

  • Gamma Spectrometry (GS): A method that identifies and quantifies radioactive materials in a sample through measurement of gamma rays emitted from those materials.
  • Gas Flow Proportional Counting (GFPC): A method that quantifies radioactive materials in a sample through measurement of high-energy alpha and/or beta radiation emitted from those materials. This method alone cannot identify the radioactive material species in the sample.
  • Liquid Scintillation Counting (LSC): A method that quantifies radioactive materials in a sample through measurement of low-energy alpha and/or beta radiation. This method alone cannot identify the radioactive material species in the sample. For the MREMP, only tritium is measured through this method.
  • Thermoluminescent Dosimetry (TLD): A method that quantifies radiation exposure to all forms of radiation. For the MREMP, TLDs are used to measure radiation exposure from x-rays and gamma rays only.

The following sample types are collected as part of the MREMP. The radionuclides or radiation type that each sample is analyzed for is provided, along with the analysis technique shown in parenthesis. In prior reports, milk sample results are referred to as Terrestrial.

  • Water: Gross Beta (GFPC), Tritium (LSC)
  • Milk: I-131 (GS), Cs-137 (GS), K-40 (GS)
  • Air: Gross Beta (GFPC), I-131 (GS)
  • Direct: Ambient Gamma (TLD)

 

Multiple samples are collected for each facility and at varying frequencies. The details for the current MREMP are provided below. Changes to the program have occurred over time. For example, Big Rock Point only has TLD monitoring for direct gamma radiation. Other environmental samples are no longer part of the program for Big Rock Point, since the plant has been decommissioned and only an independent spent fuel storage installation (ISFSI) remains.

  • Fermi (Includes Fermi 1 (shut down) and Fermi 2):
    • Water: 5 locations, 4 monthly and 1 every other week
    • Milk: 1 location, every other week
    • Air: 5 locations, continuous collection analyzed weekly
    • Direct: 16 locations, continuous collection analyzed quarterly
  • Palisades:
    • Water: 1 location, monthly
    • Milk: 1 location (shared with Cook), every other week
    • Air: 3 locations, continuous collection analyzed weekly
    • Direct: 15 locations, continuous collection analyzed quarterly
  • Big Rock Point:
    • Water: None
    • Milk: None
    • Air: None
    • Direct: 4 locations, continuous collection analyzed quarterly
  • Lansing (Background):
    • Water: 1 location, every other week
    • Milk: 1 location, every other week
    • Air: 1 location, continuous collection analyzed weekly
    • Direct: 1 location, continuous collection analyzed quarterly

Data is updated approximately on a quarterly basis. The various fields and units are provided below to assist users with interpretation. Results showing “LT” indicates that the measured amount of radiation is Less Than the laboratory minimum detection limit, which is shown after “LT”. Laboratory uncertainty is provided after the ± symbol and provides a level of confidence for the reported value. A table with field definitions is provided for each sample type. Occasionally, samples are not collected due to scheduling issues, being lost in transit, or laboratory reporting issues. For these samples, a “no result” or “lost” may be displayed.

 

Commercial Nuclear Power Plants Descriptions

Surface Water Results Descriptions


 Milk Results Descriptions


 Air Results Descriptions

Direct Radiation Descriptions

properties.snippet: Areas (fields) where oil and gas products are produced from underground reservoirs.
properties.description:
Producing oil and gas fields are symbolized based on their producing formation(s). Antrim Shale formation is unique in that it uses county-wide field designations. Updates will provided on an as needed basis. For more information, visit Michigan.gov/EGLE/Maps-Data/GeoWebFace or contact EGLE-GeoWebFace@michigan.gov.


Field NameField Descriptions
ObjectIDESRI ID Number
FieldNumberField Number
FieldNameField Name
ProducingFormationNameProducing Formation Name
ProducingFmCodeProducing Formation Code
FieldTypeType of Field code:

  • OG: Oil and Gas
  • G: Gas
  • O: Oil
  • GS: Gas Storage
  • GC: Gas Condensate
  • UNK: Unknown

DiscoveryAPI14 Digit api field discovery well
DateDiscoveredDate field discovered
DateAbandonedDate field abandoned
GlobalIDESRI ID Number

properties.snippet: Drilling Units and Uniform Spacing Plans for Permitted Oil and Gas Wells in Michigan.
properties.description:
Drilling Unit: A regulated maximum area, built from the governmentally surveyed quarter, quarter sections of land. It is the area which may be efficiently and economically drained by a well or wells.

Uniform Spacing Plans (USP): An area of land subject to S.O. 14-9-94, pooled in multiples of governmentally surveyed quarter, quarter sections, and controlled by one operator for the purpose of locating Antrim wells to minimize surface waste. Visit this page for additional information on spacing orders.

Data is updated on a regular basis as new permits are issued. For more information, visit Michigan.gov/EGLE/Maps-Data/GeoWebFace or contact EGLE-GeoWebFace@michigan.gov.

properties.snippet: The Fuel Transformation Program (FTP) provides grant funding for the replacement of qualifying diesel vehicles, vessels, and equipment with new, cleaner versions that have low to no emissions. Grants to support this transition are made available through Michigan's $64.8 million allocation of Volkswagen settlement funds. This data shows the completed and in-progress grants.
properties.description:
This data includes completed and in-progress projects funded in part by the Fuel Transformation Program. Projects selected for funding mitigate diesel emissions such as oxides of nitrogen (NOx), fine particulate matter (PM2.5), and Greenhouse Gases. For more information and detail about this program’s objectives, please visit Fuel Transformation Program. This data is featured in our Fuel Transformation Program Dashboard (item details).

This data includes projects by type (vehicle, equipment, etc), the year grants were awarded, and estimated emission reduction benefits. Emission data is provided in metric tons and illustrates expected vehicle, vessel or equipment lifetime totals. Emissions were estimated using tools and calculators provided by: Office of Transportation and Air Quality, United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Energy Systems Division, Argonne National Laboratory.

Revisions to in-progress projects or the addition of new projects will be updated annually. For questions about the content of this data, email Dan Zbozien at ZbozienD@Michigan.gov.
